summary: The July 12 scout found no newer Sunday arXiv posting batch, so it screened the latest verified July 9-10 postings. The strongest heavy-ion themes are QGP azimuthal momentum isotropization, pseudo-gauge ambiguities in spin-polarization theory, pion nuclear fragmentation functions with A and nu dependence, heavy-quark coalescence model sensitivity, and STAR fluctuation/femtoscopy highlights. The strongest transferable AI/ML themes are optimal-transport event-weight resampling, cell-level calorimeter Transformers, and leave-family-out validation for EoS surrogate inference.
strongest_paper_summary: arXiv:2607.07796 is the strongest heavy-ion paper because it directly quantifies relaxation of initial azimuthal momentum anisotropies during QGP thermalization. arXiv:2607.08723 is the strongest workflow paper because it addresses negative and pathological event weights, a concrete production bottleneck in NLO collider simulation.

papers.title: Azimuthal momentum isotropization in the Quark-Gluon Plasma thermalization
papers.authors_short: Sergio Barrera Cabodevila, Xiaojian Du, Carlos A. Salgado, Bin Wu
papers.abstract: Studies relaxation of initial azimuthal momentum anisotropies during QGP thermalization using the Boltzmann Equation in Diffusion Approximation, finding harmonic-dependent relaxation times and pT-dependent harmonic evolution relevant to small-system phenomenology.

papers.title: A symmetry-based resolution of pseudo-gauge ambiguities in local equilibrium
papers.abstract: Reformulates pseudo-gauge and current-improvement ambiguities in local-equilibrium density operators as spurious symmetries and proposes a prescription based on genuine conserved currents, with relevance to spin polarization in heavy-ion collisions.

papers.title: Analysis of Nuclear Fragmentation Functions for Pions with A and nu Dependence
papers.authors_short: Mengyang Li, Zijian Ye, Jun Gao, Xiaomin Shen, Hongxi Xing, Yuxiang Zhao
papers.abstract: Fits pion nuclear fragmentation functions with nuclear effects parameterized by mass number A, parton energy nu, and hadron energy fraction z, using SIDIS data and NLO pp/pA comparisons to ALICE.

papers.title: Heavy quark coalescence probability in the presence of a potential
papers.abstract: Studies how a phenomenological heavy-quark potential affects coalescence probability and how moderate in-medium modifications change the result.

papers.title: Recent highlights from the STAR Experiment
papers.abstract: Summarizes recent STAR results on mean-pT correlations, net-proton cumulants, identical-pion femtoscopy, baryon-strangeness correlations, and baryon-baryon femtoscopy across sqrt(sNN)=3-200 GeV.

papers.title: Optimal-Transport-Based Cell Resampling for Negative and Pathological Event Weights
papers.authors_short: Regan Doherty, Lauren Hay, Rishabh Jain, Matt LeBlanc, Julia Marrinan, Camille Mauceri, Jennifer Roloff
papers.abstract: Uses optimal-transport metrics for local resampling of negative and very large Monte Carlo event weights, reducing observed bias in NLO QCD samples relative to earlier cell-resampling techniques.

papers.title: Transformer-based machine learning using low-level calorimeter signals for collimated photon identification at collider experiments
papers.authors_short: Gabriel Matos, Lauren Larson, Abhilasha Dave, Maria Bressan, Azal Amer, et al.
papers.abstract: Compares high-level and cell-level ML methods for collimated diphoton versus isolated-photon identification, with a Transformer giving the best classification performance and direct invariant-mass regression from calorimeter cells.

papers.title: The Generalization Gap in Machine Learning EoS Inference from Core-Collapse Supernova Gravitational Waves
papers.abstract: Shows that random cross-validation can make ML EoS inference look successful, while leave-one-EoS-out validation fails badly across model classes, exposing template leakage.

papers.title: Secondary Hadron-Nucleus Collisions of Short-Lived Hadrons in Ultra-Relativistic Fixed-Target Heavy-Ion Interactions
papers.authors_short: Sanatan Digal, P. S. Saumia, Ajit M. Srivastava
papers.abstract: Argues that boosted short-lived hadrons produced in a fixed-target heavy-ion interaction may survive to collide with another nucleus in a solid target, opening unusual secondary hadron-nucleus channels.
